refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation in the problem domain without prescribing how (e.g., protocol selection, redundancy, error correction). The phrase 'customer-defined' remains because the acceptance threshold is a stakeholder-negotiated constraint, not a design choice. This formulation is solution-neutral and atomic.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission durations and workload patterns. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions and acceptance sign-off by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data integrity within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover to maintain continuous connectivity across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to reserve bandwidth for critical teleoperation commands and reduce contention-induced loss
DP4: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured packet loss rates
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with QoS enforcement
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with QoS enforcement

potential_sources: Loss detection mechanism must be decoupled from retransmission logic to allow independent tuning of detection sensitivity and retry strategies
QoS enforcement depends on network infrastructure capabilities; if unavailable, fallback to application-level buffering must be pre-defined
Adaptive buffering parameters (buffer size, timeout thresholds) must not be tightly coupled to specific network latency assumptions; parameterization required
Retransmission strategy should remain agnostic to underlying transport protocol to support multiple network conditions
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is preferred because it addresses the FR through orthogonal mechanisms: (1) loss detection and measurement, (2) adaptive response (buffering/retransmission), and (3) network-level prioritization. Each can be designed, tested, and evolved independently. Coupling risks are manageable through clear interface contracts and parameterization.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is achievable within the agreed network conditions; if baseline loss exceeds this, mitigation strategy alone may be insufficient.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands tolerate bounded latency introduced by retransmission and buffering; real-time safety-critical commands may require separate low-latency path.
RISK: Adaptive buffering may introduce variable latency that degrades operator situational awareness; operator training and UI feedback mechanisms are required.
RISK: QoS enforcement depends on network operator cooperation; in shared/public networks, QoS guarantees may not be enforceable.
RISK: Loss detection accuracy depends on reliable acknowledgment signaling; if ACK path is also lossy, detection becomes unreliable.
RISK: Retransmission overhead increases bandwidth consumption; if network is bandwidth-constrained, aggressive retransmission may worsen congestion.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits the latency and buffering delays introduced by the hybrid approach; safety-critical commands may require bypass or separate channel.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability by detecting packet loss in real-time, applying adaptive buffering and selective retransmission, and enforcing QoS prioritization such that the measured packet loss rate remains within the customer-defined acceptance criterion (≤1% or customer-specified target) under the agreed network conditions, while ensuring that latency introduced by mitigation mechanisms does not exceed operator-acceptable bounds defined in the worksite safety plan and operational policies.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote operation control logic, including network communication resilience, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, and QoS enforcement for command/feedback channels between operator and vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for transmitting operator commands and receiving vehicle telemetry; DP5 loss detection and retransmission operate at this boundary.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level mission directives from operator; DP5 QoS enforcement ensures critical tasking commands are delivered reliably.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state and sensor data to operator; DP5 adaptive buffering manages telemetry flow under congestion.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates safety-critical events when network QoS degrades below thresholds; triggers failsafe behavior.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are core teleoperation concerns. QoS enforcement directly manages command/feedback reliability. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation requires external notification, but logic remains contained.

Poor fit: DP5 is fundamentally about remote communication resilience, not vehicle actuation or onboard sensing. Placing network-layer logic in Vehicle creates tight coupling to communication infrastructure and violates separation of concerns. High diffusion to Teleoperation and Network Link.

Weak fit: while QoS enforcement has safety implications, the core DP (loss detection, buffering, retransmission) is not a safety service responsibility. Safety Services should consume QoS status, not implement network resilience. Significant diffusion to Teleoperation and Network Link.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP5 here bypasses architectural layering and creates diffusion across all subsystems. No clear ownership or interface boundaries.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) is fundamentally a teleoperation communication resilience strategy. It operates on the command/feedback channels between operator and vehicle, manages network congestion and packet loss, and enforces service quality for remote control. Teleoperation is the natural owner because: (1) it owns the Network Link interface where loss occurs; (2) it manages Tasking ingress and Telemetry egress, both subject to buffering and QoS; (3) it can escalate SafetyAlert when QoS degrades; (4) loss detection and retransmission logic are cohesive with remote operation concerns, not vehicle or safety-specific logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystem has its own onboard network resilience (e.g., inter-module communication), DP5 logic may need to be replicated or coordinated, creating diffusion. Mitigation: Clarify that DP5 applies to operator-vehicle link only; onboard resilience is Vehicle's internal concern.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation requires tight coupling to Safety Services. If safety thresholds are not well-defined, QoS enforcement logic may become entangled with safety policy. Mitigation: Define clear QoS-to-safety mapping (e.g., 'if latency > X ms, trigger SafetyAlert') and keep policy external to Teleoperation.

Risk: Adaptive buffering strategy may conflict with Vehicle's real-time constraints if feedback latency becomes unpredictable. Mitigation: Establish latency SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle; buffer only non-critical telemetry.

Risk: Selective retransmission logic requires knowledge of packet priority/criticality, which may be defined by Tasking or Safety Services. Mitigation: Teleoperation should receive priority metadata from upstream, not infer it.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions) is measurable and directly traceable to DP5 implementation within the Teleoperation module. Loss detection and retransmission logic can be instrumented to report packet loss rates; adaptive buffering and QoS enforcement can be validated through network simulation and field testing under controlled degradation scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation module owns the operator-vehicle communication link and all associated resilience mechanisms. Interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined. Ownership boundary is unambiguous with a containment score of 0.92, indicating strong architectural fit.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the packet loss acceptance criterion. The DP is logically sound: loss detection identifies failures, adaptive buffering absorbs transient congestion, selective retransmission recovers critical packets, and QoS enforcement prioritizes command delivery. The approach is coherent and sufficient to achieve the stated acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Onboard vehicle network resilience (inter-module communication within Vehicle subsystem) may require similar loss detection and buffering logic. If Vehicle implements its own resilience independently, DP5 concepts will diffuse across two modules without coordination. Mitigation: Establish clear boundary—DP5 applies exclusively to operator-vehicle link; Vehicle's internal communication resilience is a separate concern with its own FR and DP.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation creates a dependency on Safety Services' threshold definitions and alert handling. If safety policy (e.g., 'QoS degradation triggers failsafe') is not externalized, QoS enforcement logic may become entangled with safety decision-making. Mitigation: Define QoS-to-safety mapping as a configuration artifact external to Teleoperation; Teleoperation reports QoS status, Safety Services interprets and acts.

Risk: Selective retransmission requires knowledge of packet priority/criticality (e.g., 'emergency stop' vs. 'telemetry update'). If priority metadata is inferred within Teleoperation rather than received from Tasking or Safety Services, the module will diffuse responsibility for priority policy. Mitigation: Require Tasking interface to supply priority tags with each command; Teleoperation uses tags to guide retransmission strategy, not to define priority.

Risk: Adaptive buffering strategy may introduce unpredictable latency in telemetry feedback, conflicting with Vehicle's real-time control loops if Vehicle expects bounded latency. Mitigation: Establish explicit latency SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle; buffer only non-critical telemetry; reserve low-latency path for safety-critical feedback.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment within Teleoperation module (score 0.92). Loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are cohesive network resilience concerns that naturally belong to the module owning the operator-vehicle communication link. QoS enforcement is a direct responsibility of Teleoperation because it manages both ingress (Tasking) and egress (Telemetry) flows. Minor diffusion (0.08) is limited to SafetyAlert escalation, which is a notification interface rather than a logic diffusion—Teleoperation detects QoS degradation and signals Safety Services, but does not implement safety policy. This is appropriate separation of concerns. Alternative placements (Vehicle, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) show poor fit (0.35–0.75 diffusion) because they lack ownership of the operator-vehicle link or would violate separation of concerns by mixing communication resilience with vehicle actuation or safety policy.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Externalize QoS-to-safety mapping. Define a configuration file or interface contract that specifies thresholds (e.g., 'if latency > 500 ms, trigger SafetyAlert') outside Teleoperation logic. Teleoperation reports QoS metrics; Safety Services consumes them and decides escalation. This prevents entanglement of communication resilience with safety policy.

Revision 2: Require priority metadata from Tasking interface. Modify the Tasking interface contract to include a priority field (e.g., CRITICAL, HIGH, NORMAL) for each command. Teleoperation uses this metadata to guide selective retransmission, rather than inferring priority internally. This clarifies responsibility: Tasking defines what is critical, Teleoperation ensures delivery.

Revision 3: Establish latency SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Document maximum acceptable latency for telemetry feedback (e.g., 'safety-critical feedback ≤ 100 ms, non-critical ≤ 500 ms'). Use these SLAs to configure adaptive buffering thresholds. This prevents Teleoperation's buffering strategy from violating Vehicle's real-time constraints.

Revision 4: Clarify scope boundary for onboard resilience. Add a note to the Teleoperation module specification: 'DP5 applies to operator-vehicle communication link only. Vehicle subsystem is responsible for its own internal network resilience (inter-module communication). These are separate concerns with independent FRs and DPs.' This prevents diffusion of loss detection and buffering logic across modules.

Revision 5: Define acceptance test plan for packet loss criterion. Specify test scenarios (e.g., simulated packet loss at 2%, 5%, 10%; network congestion; latency jitter) and measurement methodology (e.g., packet counters, end-to-end latency histograms). Ensure tests validate that DP5 achieves ≤ 1% effective packet loss under agreed network conditions. This makes the FR-DP-IM chain verifiable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the FR is sufficiently specific to be implementable without further decomposition. However, the gate analysis identifies four concrete diffusion risks and recommends five targeted revisions that must be applied before proceeding to implementation. The safety-critical context (high criticality) amplifies the importance of externalizing QoS-to-safety mapping and establishing explicit latency SLAs. Revising the current level to incorporate these mitigations will eliminate ambiguity, prevent architectural diffusion, and ensure the FR-DP-IM chain is verifiable and maintainable. Once revisions are applied, the FR is ready for detailed design and implementation without further decomposition.

revise.why: DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) is logically sound but requires explicit refinement to address diffusion risks and safety-critical constraints. Revisions must clarify: (1) QoS-to-safety mapping is externalized and not embedded in Teleoperation logic; (2) priority metadata for selective retransmission is supplied by Tasking interface, not inferred internally; (3) adaptive buffering respects explicit latency SLAs to avoid violating Vehicle's real-time constraints; (4) scope boundary clearly excludes onboard vehicle network resilience. These refinements strengthen the DP specification without changing its core logic, ensuring it remains coherent, contained, and verifiable.

assumptions: Tasking interface can be extended to supply priority metadata (CRITICAL, HIGH, NORMAL) with each command without breaking existing contracts.

Safety Services can consume QoS metrics (latency, packet loss, buffer depth) from Teleoperation and apply externalized policy rules to decide escalation, without requiring Teleoperation to embed safety thresholds.
Vehicle subsystem has or will define its own FR and DP for internal network resilience (inter-module communication), independent of operator-vehicle link resilience.
Latency SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle can be established and validated through simulation and field testing under controlled network degradation.

Packet loss acceptance criterion (≤ 1% under agreed network conditions) is measurable via instrumentation of loss detection and retransmission logic, and can be validated in both simulation and field environments.
open_risks: Risk: If Tasking interface does not currently support priority metadata, adding it may require backward-compatibility handling or phased rollout. Mitigation: Define priority field as optional with default value (e.g., NORMAL); implement graceful degradation in Teleoperation if priority is absent.

Risk: QoS-to-safety mapping externalization requires coordination with Safety Services team. If safety policy is not formalized or is embedded in legacy code, externalizing it may face organizational resistance. Mitigation: Establish cross-team working group early; document safety policy as configuration artifact in design review.

Risk: Latency SLAs may conflict with adaptive buffering strategy under high congestion. If network is severely degraded, buffering to ensure delivery may exceed latency SLA, forcing a choice between reliability and responsiveness. Mitigation: Define SLA trade-off rules (e.g., 'safety-critical feedback always respects latency SLA; non-critical telemetry may buffer up to SLA limit'). Escalate to Safety Services if SLA cannot be met.

Risk: Selective retransmission based on priority may introduce fairness issues if high-priority packets consistently displace low-priority ones, starving non-critical telemetry. Mitigation: Implement weighted fair queuing or priority-aware congestion control to balance reliability and fairness.

Risk: Verification of packet loss criterion under 'agreed network conditions' requires clear definition of test scenarios and network models. If conditions are not precisely specified, acceptance testing may be ambiguous. Mitigation: Define test plan (Revision 5) with explicit scenarios (packet loss rates, latency jitter, congestion patterns) and measurement methodology before implementation.
evidence_needed: Tasking interface specification update: Confirm priority field can be added; document backward-compatibility strategy.
Safety Services interface specification: Confirm QoS metrics (latency, packet loss, buffer depth) can be reported; document expected format and update frequency.
Vehicle subsystem FR and DP for internal network resilience: Confirm scope boundary is clear and independent of operator-vehicle link resilience.
Latency SLA document: Specify maximum acceptable latency for safety-critical feedback (e.g., ≤ 100 ms) and non-critical telemetry (e.g., ≤ 500 ms); document trade-off rules for congestion scenarios.

Acceptance test plan (Revision 5): Define test scenarios (simulated packet loss at 2%, 5%, 10%; network congestion; latency jitter), measurement methodology (packet counters, end-to-end latency histograms), and success criteria (effective packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed conditions).

QoS-to-safety mapping configuration artifact: Define format (e.g., JSON, YAML, or database table) and ownership (Safety Services or shared configuration management); document thresholds and escalation rules.
Network simulation environment: Confirm availability of tools to model agreed network conditions and validate DP5 under controlled degradation scenarios.
